950	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient class and its Hilbert function}
951	
952	\begin{definition}\leanok
953	[Subquotient Hilbert data]
954	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}
955	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotientHilb}
956	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
957	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
958	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e. \(M\) together with an
959	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
960	  (\(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\ \mathcal{M}\)) whose components
961	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
962	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
963	  \begin{itemize}
964	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
965	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
966	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
967	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
968	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
969	    \item a finiteness witness that \(M\) is a finite module over the polynomial
970	      ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\)
971	      (\(\mathrm{Module.Finite}\)).
972	  \end{itemize}
973	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
974	  Hilbert function} is
975	  \[
976	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
977	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
978	  \]
979	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
980	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
981	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
982	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
983	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
984	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
985	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
986	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
987	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
988	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
989	\end{definition}
990	
991	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
992	
993	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
994	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
995	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
996	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
997	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
998	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
999	
1000	\begin{definition}[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1001	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1002	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1003	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1004	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1005	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1006	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1007	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1008	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1009	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1010	\end{definition}
1011	
1012	\begin{lemma}[Membership form of degree-raising]
1013	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1014	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1015	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1016	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1017	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1018	\end{lemma}
1019	
1020	\begin{proof}
1021	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1022	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1023	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1024	\end{proof}
1025	
1026	\begin{lemma}[Degree-shift commutation]
1027	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1028	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1029	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1030	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1031	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1032	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1033	  \[
1034	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1035	  \]
1036	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1037	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1038	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1039	\end{lemma}
1040	
1041	\begin{proof}
1042	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1043	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous
1044	  components and apply \(x\): \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with
1045	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1046	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1047	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1048	\end{proof}
1049	
1050	\begin{lemma}[Degree-raising kills the bottom]
1051	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1052	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1053	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1054	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then the degree-\(0\) component of \(x m\) vanishes
1055	  for every \(m\): \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0\).
1056	\end{lemma}
1057	
1058	\begin{proof}
1059	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1060	  Writing \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with each summand in
1061	  \(\mathcal{M}_{j+1}\), no summand has degree \(0\) (every \(j+1 \ge 1\)), so the
1062	  degree-\(0\) component is \(0\).
1063	\end{proof}
1064	
1065	\begin{lemma}[Preimage of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1066	  \label{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1067	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_isHomogeneous}
1068	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1069	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N' \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1070	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the preimage \(x^{-1}(N') =
1071	  \{m : x m \in N'\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of \(M\).
1072	\end{lemma}
1073	
1074	\begin{proof}
1075	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1076	  Let \(m \in x^{-1}(N')\) and fix a degree \(i\). By the degree-shift commutation
1077	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}), \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)) =
1078	  \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m)\), which lies in \(N'\) because \(x m \in N'\) and \(N'\)
1079	  is homogeneous. Hence \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\), so \(x^{-1}(N')\) is
1080	  homogeneous.
1081	\end{proof}
1082	
1083	\begin{lemma}[Image of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1084	  \label{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1085	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_isHomogeneous}
1086	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1087	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1088	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule.
1089	  Then the image \(x \cdot N = \{x m : m \in N\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of
1090	  \(M\), with degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(x(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) and zero
1091	  degree-\(0\) piece.
1092	\end{lemma}
1093	
1094	\begin{proof}
1095	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1096	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1097	  Take \(x m \in x \cdot N\) with \(m \in N\) and fix a degree. In degree \(0\) the
1098	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0 \in x \cdot N\)
1099	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}). In degree \(i+1\),
1100	  \(\mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\)
1101	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); since \(N\) is homogeneous,
1102	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in N\), so this component lies in \(x \cdot N\). Thus every
1103	  homogeneous component of \(x m\) lies in \(x \cdot N\), which is therefore
1104	  homogeneous.
1105	\end{proof}
1106	
1107	\begin{lemma}[Intersection of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1108	  \label{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}
1109	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.inf_isHomogeneous}
1110	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1111	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules
1112	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) then so is their intersection \(p
1113	  \cap q\). Mathlib provides no lattice-closure lemmas for homogeneous submodules, so
1114	  this is recorded here.
1115	\end{lemma}
1116	
1117	\begin{proof}
1118	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1119	  For \(m \in p \cap q\) and any degree \(i\), homogeneity of \(p\) and of \(q\)
1120	  gives \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in q\), hence
1121	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p \cap q\).
1122	\end{proof}
1123	
1124	\begin{lemma}[Sum of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1125	  \label{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1126	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_isHomogeneous}
1127	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1128	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules then so is their sum \(p + q\).
1129	\end{lemma}
1130	
1131	\begin{proof}
1132	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1133	  Write an element of \(p + q\) as \(a + b\) with \(a \in p\), \(b \in q\). For any
1134	  degree \(i\), \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) = \mathrm{proj}_i(a) + \mathrm{proj}_i(b)\)
1135	  with \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(b) \in q\) by homogeneity,
1136	  so \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) \in p + q\).
1137	\end{proof}
1138	
1139	\begin{lemma}[Ambient image identity]
1140	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1142	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1143	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1144	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1145	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1146	  \[
1147	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1148	  \]
1149	\end{lemma}
1150	
1151	\begin{proof}
1152	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1153	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1154	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1155	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1156	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1157	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1158	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1159	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1160	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1161	\end{proof}
1162	
1163	\begin{lemma}[Ambient distributive law]
1164	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1166	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1167	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1168	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1169	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1170	  \[
1171	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1172	  \]
1173	\end{lemma}
1174	
1175	\begin{proof}
1176	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1177	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1178	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1179	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1180	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1181	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1182	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1183	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1184	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1185	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1186	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1187	\end{proof}

1189	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1190	
1191	\begin{lemma}[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1192	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_ker_coker}
1194	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1195	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1196	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1197	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1198	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1199	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1200	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1201	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1202	  \[
1203	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1204	    \qquad
1205	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1206	  \]
1207	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1208	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1209	  \begin{enumerate}
1210	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1211	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1212	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1213	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1214	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1215	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1216	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1217	  \end{enumerate}
1218	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1219	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1220	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1221	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1222	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1223	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1224	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1225	\end{lemma}
1226	
1227	\begin{proof}
1228	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1229	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1230	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1231	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1232	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1233	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1234	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1235	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1236	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1237	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1238	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1239	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1240	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1241	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1242	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1243	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1244	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1245	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1246	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1247	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1248	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1249	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1250	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1251	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1252	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1253	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1254	
1255	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1256	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1257	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1258	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1259	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1260	
1261	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1262	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1263	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1264	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1265	
1266	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1267	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1268	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1269	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1270	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1271	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1272	\end{proof}

1274	\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}
1275	
1276	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1277	[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
1278	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1279	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1280	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1281	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1282	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1283	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1284	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1285	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1286	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1287	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1288	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1289	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1290	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1291	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1292	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1293	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1294	  \[
1295	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1296	  \]
1297	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1298	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1299	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1300	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1301	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1302	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1303	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1304	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1305	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1306	\end{lemma}
1307	
1308	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1309	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1310	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1311	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1312	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1313	%  following short exact sequence
1314	%  $$
1315	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1316	%  $$
1317	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1318	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1319	\begin{proof}
1320	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1321	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1322	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1323	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1324	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1325	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1326	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1327	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1328	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1329	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1330	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1331	
1332	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1333	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1334	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1335	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1336	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1337	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1338	
1339	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1340	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1341	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1342	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1343	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1344	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1345	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1346	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1347	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1348	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1349	
1350	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1351	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1352	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1353	  \[
1354	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1355	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1356	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1357	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1358	  \]
1359	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1360	\end{proof}
1361	
1362	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1363	
1364	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness transfer for the subquotients]
1365	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1366	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1367	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1368	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1369	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r \ge 1\)
1370	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), so that \(M\) is finite over
1371	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Then the two
1372	  subquotients
1373	  \[
1374	    K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N'), \qquad C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)
1375	  \]
1376	  of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} are finite modules over
1377	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\); that is, they are subquotient data of length
1378	  \(r-1\) with a valid \(\mathrm{Module.Finite}\) witness. This is the finiteness
1379	  needed for the inductive hypothesis of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}.
1380	
1381	  \emph{The datum as a single bundle.} The subquotient datum -- the homogeneous pair
1382	  \(N' \le N\) together with the \(r\) pairwise-commuting degree-raising
1383	  endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1384	  t_{r-1}]\)-module structure on \(M\) below, its scalar-tower compatibility with the
1385	  \(\kappa\)-action, and the \(\mathrm{Module.Finite}\) witness -- is naturally
1386	  carried as one bundle, so that the kernel \(K\) and cokernel \(C\) of
1387	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} are handed back as bundles of the same
1388	  shape but length \(r-1\).
1389	\end{lemma}
1390	
1391	\begin{proof}
1392	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1393	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1394	  \emph{The polynomial module structure on \(M\).} The \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1395	  degree-raising endomorphisms \(x_0, \dots, x_{r-1}\) generate a \(\kappa\)-subalgebra
1396	  \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots, x_{r-1}\}\) of the \emph{noncommutative}
1397	  endomorphism algebra \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); because the generators
1398	  commute pairwise, \(A\) is commutative (\cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}).
1399	  Commutativity of \(A\) is exactly what makes the evaluation homomorphism at the
1400	  generators a legal \(\kappa\)-algebra homomorphism \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1401	  \to A\): evaluation refuses the noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) as a
1402	  target. Composing with the inclusion \(A \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1403	  and restricting scalars along it endows \(M\) with the structure of a module over
1404	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action and
1405	  characterised by \(t_i \cdot m = x_i m\). This is the \(\mathrm{Module.Finite}\)
1406	  carrier recorded in the datum.
1407	
1408	  \emph{Transfer down one generator.} Set \(A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) and
1409	  \(x = t_{r-1}\). Since \(M\) is finite over the Noetherian ring \(A\), \(M\) is a
1410	  Noetherian \(A\)-module, so the submodule \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq M\) is
1411	  finite over \(A\) and the quotients \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\) and
1412	  \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) are finite over \(A\). By
1413	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} the element \(x = t_{r-1}\) annihilates
1414	  both subquotients, so the \(A\)-action on each factors through the surjection of
1415	  polynomial rings
1416	  \[
1417	    A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]
1418	  \]
1419	  fixing \(t_i\) for \(i < r-1\) and sending \(t_{r-1} \mapsto 0\). Applying the
1420	  finite-generation transfer along a surjection
1421	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}) turns finiteness over \(A\)
1422	  into finiteness over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\), supplying the length-\((r-1)\)
1423	  finiteness witnesses for \(K\) and \(C\).
1424	
1425	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1426	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\), not the subalgebra
1427	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots, x_{r-2}\} \subseteq
1428	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1429	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection that
1430	  the scalar transfer requires. Keeping free polynomial rings at every step keeps the
1431	  surjection -- and hence the transfer -- available throughout. Everything is finite
1432	  generation of ambient submodules of the fixed \(M\) and their quotients; no
1433	  derived-carrier grading is used.
1434	\end{proof}
1435	
1436	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
1437	
1438	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1439	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
1440	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
1441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
1442	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1443	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1444	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
1445	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
1446	  \[
1447	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
1448	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
1449	  \]
1450	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
1451	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
1452	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
1453	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
1454	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
1455	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
1456	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
1457	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
1458	  \[
1459	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
1460	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
1461	  \]
1462	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
1463	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
1464	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
1465	\end{lemma}
1466	
1467	\begin{proof}
1468	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1469	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1470	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
1471	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
1472	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
1473	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
1474	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
1475	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
1476	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
1477	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
1478	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
1479	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
1480	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
1481	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
1482	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
1483	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
1484	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
1485	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
1486	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
1487	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
1488	\end{proof}
1489	
1490	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient induction}
1491	
1492	\begin{lemma}[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
1493	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
1494	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
1495	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1496	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
1497	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
1498	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump}
1499	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1500	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1501	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
1502	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
1503	  degree-one generators).}
1504	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
1505	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
1506	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
1507	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1508	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
1509	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
1510	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
1511	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
1512	  \(M\).
1513	\end{lemma}
1514	
1515	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
1516	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
1517	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
1518	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
1519	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
1520	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
1521	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
1522	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
1523	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
1524	\begin{proof}
1525	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1526	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
1527	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
1528	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump}
1529	  We induct on the length \(r\).
1530	
1531	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} With no endomorphisms, the finiteness witness says
1532	  \(M\) is a finite module over \(\kappa[\,] = \kappa\), so \(M\) is a
1533	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space. Hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = 0\) and
1534	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n = 0\) for all \(n \gg 0\), so \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for
1535	  \(n \gg 0\). By \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is
1536	  rational of order \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
1537	
1538	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
1539	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
1540	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
1541	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), with valid
1542	  finiteness witnesses supplied by the finiteness transfer
1543	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The induction hypothesis applied
1544	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
1545	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
1546	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
1547	  \[
1548	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
1549	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
1550	  \]
1551	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
1552	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
1553	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
1554	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
1555	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
1556	\end{proof}

1558	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
1559	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
1560	
1561	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
1562	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
1563	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
1564	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
1565	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
1566	
1567	\begin{definition}
1568	
1569	\leanok
1570	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
1571	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
1572	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
1573	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1574	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
1575	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
1576	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
1577	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
1578	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
1579	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
1580	  % Polynomials" (read from
1581	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
1582	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1583	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
1584	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
1585	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
1586	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
1587	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
1588	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
1589	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
1590	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
1591	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
1592	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
1593	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
1594	  \(U \subseteq X\),
1595	  \[
1596	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
1597	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
1598	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
1599	  \]
1600	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
1601	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
1602	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
1603	
1604	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
1605	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
1606	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
1607	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
1608	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
1609	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
1610	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
1611	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
1612	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
1613	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
1614	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
1615	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1616	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
1617	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
1618	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
1619	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1620	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
1621	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
1622	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
1623	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
1624	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
1625	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
1626	
1627	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
1628	\end{definition}

1630	\begin{lemma}\leanok

1651	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1652	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
1653	  localization]
1654	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
1655	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
1656	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
1657	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
1658	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
1659	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
1660	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
1661	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
1662	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
1663	  \[
1664	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
1665	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
1666	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
1667	  \]
1668	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
1669	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
1670	
1671	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
1672	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
1673	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
1674	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
1675	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
1676	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
1677	\end{lemma}
1678	
1679	\begin{proof}
1680	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
1681	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
1682	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
1683	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
1684	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
1685	
1686	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
1687	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
1688	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
1689	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
1690	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
1691	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
1692	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
1693	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
1694	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
1695	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
1696	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
1697	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
1698	\end{proof}

1700	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
1701	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1702	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
1703	  \mathlibok
1704	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
1705	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
1706	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
1707	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
1708	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
1709	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
1710	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
1711	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
1712	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
1713	\end{lemma}

1715	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
1716	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1717	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
1718	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1719	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
1720	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
1721	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
1722	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1723	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
1724	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
1725	  %  associated to $M$.
1726	  %  [...]
1727	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
1728	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
1729	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
1730	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
1731	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
1732	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
1733	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
1734	  \begin{enumerate}
1735	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
1736	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
1737	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
1738	    \item the section-restriction map
1739	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
1740	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
1741	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
1742	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
1743	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
1744	  \end{enumerate}
1745	\end{lemma}
1746	
1747	\begin{proof}
1748	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1749	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
1750	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
1751	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
1752	
1753	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
1754	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
1755	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
1756	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
1757	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
1758	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
1759	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
1760	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
1761	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
1762	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
1763	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
1764	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
1765	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
1766	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
1767	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
1768	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
1769	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
1770	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
1771	\end{proof}

1826	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
1827	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
1828	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
1829	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
1830	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
1831	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
